metadata.title: The Daily Reflection Tree
metadata.author: Prakash Kumar Prajapati
metadata.description: A deterministic end-of-day reflection tool covering three psychological axes: Locus, Orientation, and Radius.

start.text: Good evening. This is your space — no performance, no judgment. Let's take a quiet look at your day.

q_energy_check.text: Before we dive in — how would you describe today in one word or phrase?

options.text: Exhausting
Productive
Frustrating
Steady — nothing dramatic

q_locus_friction.text: You said '{q_energy_check}'. When something didn't go as planned today, what was your first instinct?

options.text: Figure out what I could still control
Feel frustrated by things outside my control
Wait and see if things sorted themselves out
Adapt and find another way through

q_locus_impact.text: You said '{q_energy_check}'. When something went well today, what made it happen?

options.text: I prepared well and stayed focused
Honestly, the timing just worked out
I adapted quickly when the situation shifted
The team came through — I relied on others

q_locus_choice.text: Think of one moment today where things got difficult. Looking back — did you have any choice in how you responded?

options.text: Yes — I could have responded differently and I knew it
Not really — the situation left me no room
Maybe — I didn't stop to think about it at the time

r_locus_victim.text: You described today as '{q_energy_check}'. A day like that pulls attention outward — to what others did or didn't do, to what the situation allowed or didn't. That's a fair response. But somewhere in there, you made at least one call. Even waiting is a choice.

r_locus_victor.text: You described today as '{q_energy_check}' — and you stayed in the driver's seat through it. That's not a small thing. Seeing the link between your choices and outcomes is exactly what separates learning from just surviving a day.

bridge_locus_orientation.text: Now that we've looked at how you moved through the day — let's look at where your energy was directed.

q_orientation_focus.text: In your interactions today, which of these thoughts feels most honest?

options.text: I hope the effort I put in was noticed
I hope my work moved something forward for the team
I was mostly focused on getting through my own list
I kept looking for where I could be most useful

q_entitlement_check.text: When things didn't go your way today, what was your internal reaction?

options.text: I felt I deserved more support than I received
I looked for ways to adjust my own approach to the situation.
I felt frustrated that others weren't pulling their weight

q_contribution_check.text: Was there a moment today where you helped someone with something that wasn't strictly your responsibility?

options.text: Yes — and it felt like the right thing to do
It felt like a fair trade for the effort I put in.
I thought about it but didn't act on it

q_orientation_extra.text: If you're being honest — at the end of today, what felt more important to you?

options.text: That my work was recognized and valued
That my work actually helped something or someone

r_orientation_entitled.text: It's natural to want recognition — we all carry that need. But when 'what did I receive today' becomes louder than 'what did I give today', the work starts to feel hollow. That gap is worth noticing.

r_orientation_contributor.text: You're drawing satisfaction from the act of contributing — not from the applause that follows. That's a quietly powerful place to work from. The people around you feel it, even when they don't say it.

bridge_orientation_radius.text: One last shift — let's zoom out and look at who was in your frame today.

q_radius_scope.text: Think about the most meaningful thing you did today. Who were you ultimately doing it for?

options.text: Primarily to clear my own backlog
For a specific colleague or teammate
For a user or customer I may never meet
For the larger mission we're working toward

q_radius_narrow.text: After you finished that task, what was the first thing on your mind?

options.text: What's next on my list
Whether the person receiving this would find it useful
Whether this moved the needle for the team

q_radius_wide.text: When you hit a hard moment today, who came to mind?

options.text: Just me — it was my problem to solve
My team — we were all affected by this
A specific colleague who had it harder than me
The end user or customer we're serving

q_radius_extra.text: Did you check in on anyone today — not because it was required, but because you noticed they might need it?

options.text: Yes — and it mattered to them
No — I was too focused on my own work
I noticed but didn't act on it

r_radius_self.text: Today, your frame was tight — focused on your own execution and immediate responsibilities. That's not wrong. But the most meaningful work tends to happen when we occasionally look up from our own list and ask who else is affected by what we do.

r_radius_altro.text: You kept others in your frame today — a colleague, a user, the mission. That kind of perspective-taking is what turns individual effort into collective progress. It's easy to miss. You didn't.

summary_node.text:
Today's reflection: You moved through a '{q_energy_check}' day with a {locus} orientation — seeing your work through a {orientation} lens, and holding a {radius} view of your impact.

These aren't fixed traits. They're today's snapshot. Come back tomorrow and see what shifts.

r_locus_choice_victim.text: Not seeing a choice in that moment is honest. The awareness that you're noticing it now — that's already different from just moving on.

r_locus_choice_victor.text: Even in a tough day, you found the choice point. That's not a small thing — most people don't pause long enough to see it.

end.text: That's it for today. Rest well — tomorrow is a fresh page.
